attributes.status: apocryphal angels; condemned as demons by synod

entities.raw_quote: The Book of Enoch adds Uriel, Raguel, Seraqael and Haniel… Adalbert was condemned… for praying to Uriel, Raguel, Tubuel, Adin, Tubuas, Sabaok and Samiel

Althes, Cormes, Dies, Diles, Dilia, Fabar, Felsmes, Mithiomo, Molbet, Natheus, Onaris, Pist, Progemon, Thobar, Vmon, Vralchim

associated with condemned belief in intermediate spirits

summary: This chunk discusses the spirit taxonomy and naming practices in the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), distinguishing between fallen demons, unfallen angels, and intermediate spirits. It lists 189 named spirits, only ~17% of which are well-attested elsewhere. The text explores theological tensions between orthodox demonology (fallen angels) and alternative daimonological concepts (neutral or benevolent spirits), referencing figures like Orient, Belial, Bartha, Lytim, and various demons with variant/corrupt names. It also notes condemnations of apocryphal angel invocations.
key_passages: Altogether 189 spirits are addressed by name in the Munich handbook’s conjurations, of whom 88 are referred to expressly as demones.
Bartha is a king; Baltim, Galtim, and Saltim are dukes; Belial is ‘foremost prince’; seven demons are ‘subjects’ of Lytim.

In two experiments the spirit addressed is named Orient… ‘O benign Orient, maker of the greater part of the world, heaven, and earth, at whose command all things on heaven and on earth were wisely made’.
Of the eleven spirits named in experiment no. 34, only Curson has broad currency (unless Hanni is equated with Haniel).
‘these spirits are between good and evil, dwelling neither in Hell nor in Paradise.’
